name: Amazon Q Developer CLI

form_factor.comment: Standalone terminal CLI (`q chat`). Amazon Q Developer also ships as IDE extensions/plugins, but those are a separate surface not covered by this entry. Per-form-factor links: CLI: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazonq/latest/qdeveloper-ug/command-line-installing.html.

released_in.comment: Enhanced agentic `q chat` CLI experience (multi-turn, tool use, Claude 3.7 Sonnet reasoning) announced March 6, 2025. Earlier non-agentic CLI completions predate this, but agentic chat is the surface tracked here.

latest_major_update.comment: v1.19.7 (Nov 17, 2025) is the last tagged release before the project transitioned to the closed-source Kiro CLI; AWS has since announced Amazon Q Developer end-of-support with migration to Kiro CLI through 2026-2027.

rules.comment: Markdown rule files in `.amazonq/rules/` are auto-scanned and applied per project. No AGENTS.md support (open feature request, GitHub issue #2712, unimplemented). Location: `.amazonq/rules/*.md`, referenced via agent config `resources`.

skills.comment: Custom agent JSON configs (prompt, tools, resources, hooks) act as reusable, switchable capability profiles, but there is no dedicated 'skills' folder/format distinct from agent configs. Location: agent config files, typically under `.amazonq/cli-agents/` or `~/.aws/amazonq/cli-agents/`.

hooks.comment: Agent config `hooks` field supports agentSpawn, userPromptSubmit, preToolUse, postToolUse, and stop lifecycle hooks that run shell commands and inject output into context.

mcp_servers.comment: Supports both local (stdio) and remote (HTTP/OAuth) MCP servers via global `~/.aws/amazonq/mcp.json` and workspace `.amazonq/mcp.json`, merged with workspace taking precedence on conflicts.

custom_commands.comment: No first-class user-defined slash command system; closest equivalent is agent-config hooks/prompts and `/agent create` to switch agent profiles with different tool/resource sets.

goal_command.comment: No built-in `/goal`-style persistent-objective auto-continue loop; sessions proceed turn-by-turn or via `--no-interactive` single-shot prompts.

subagents.comment: Custom agents are independent, user-selected configuration profiles (one active per session); there is no delegation/spawning of subagents from within a running session.

monitor.comment: No Monitor-equivalent found in reviewed CLI docs. Amazon Q Developer CLI supports lifecycle hooks and custom agent profiles, but not a documented background command/subagent monitor that wakes the session on completion or output.

transcripts.comment: Conversation history is auto-saved per working directory to support `--resume`, but there is no documented user-facing export/transcript file format akin to a JSONL log.

model_selection.comment: Switch models in-session with `/model`, at launch with `q chat --model <name>`, or set a default via `q settings chat.defaultModel <name>`. Choices are limited to AWS-hosted Claude models (3.5/3.7 Sonnet, Sonnet 4, Sonnet 4.5, and an 'auto' option) served over Bedrock.

approval_mode.comment: Per-tool trust model: only `fs_read` is trusted by default; `/tools trust`, `/tools trust-all`, and CLI flags `--trust-all-tools`/`--trust-tools=<list>` control auto-approval, with `/tools reset` to restore defaults.

sandbox_mode.comment: No filesystem/network sandbox; security guidance instead recommends running in a dedicated environment and using tool-trust controls (`/tools untrust`) to gate risky actions like `use_aws` or `execute_bash`.

resume.comment: `q chat --resume` automatically picks up the most recent conversation saved for the current working directory.

continue.comment: `--resume` continues the latest session for the current directory without needing an explicit session ID, satisfying both resume and continue behavior.

non_interactive.comment: `q chat --no-interactive "<prompt>"` runs a single headless turn and exits; commonly paired with `--trust-all-tools` to avoid approval prompts in scripts/CI. Some early versions had bugs around prompts still appearing or trusted-tools lists being ignored (GitHub issues #1951, #1995), later patched (e.g. v2.0.1 fix notes).

output_format.comment: `q chat` only emits plain text/Markdown even in `--no-interactive` mode; structured `-f json|json-pretty` output exists only for other subcommands like `q settings`/`q whoami`, not for chat. A JSON/stream-JSON chat output format is an open, unimplemented feature request (issue #2713).

statusline.comment: No user-configurable status line; `q settings` covers behavior toggles (telemetry, default model) rather than a persistent status bar.

telemetry.comment: Telemetry/data-sharing is user-configurable via `q settings telemetry.enabled true|false`; Free tier content may be used for service improvement, Pro tier content is excluded by policy.

keyboard_shortcuts.comment: A handful of fixed shortcuts exist (Ctrl+D to exit, Ctrl+S for slash-command fuzzy search) but there is no user-customizable keybinding configuration.

theme_customization.comment: No documented theme/color customization for the terminal chat UI.

auto_save.comment: Conversations are automatically saved per working directory without user action, enabling `--resume`.

debugging_output.comment: CLI reference documents a `--verbose` style flag and `/tools` introspection, but there is no dedicated structured debug/trace log format.

context_window.comment: `/usage` shows estimated context window consumption and `/compact` summarizes history to free space, but the window size itself is fixed by the selected model, not user-configurable.

code_formatting.comment: No built-in formatter/linter integration; any formatting happens only if the agent invokes external tools (e.g. via `execute_bash`) itself.

git_integration.comment: No dedicated git tool/UI; git operations happen through the general `execute_bash` tool like any other shell command, subject to the same trust/approval rules.

performance_mode.comment: No documented caching/performance-tuning controls exposed to users beyond model choice and context compaction.

ui_customization.comment: Single-pane terminal chat UI; no panel/layout customization since this is a CLI, not an IDE surface.

hosted_agent.comment: Runs as a local terminal process talking to the AWS-hosted backend for inference; there is no separate hosted/cloud agent execution platform for this CLI surface.

custom_model_provider.comment: Model choice is restricted to a fixed list of Claude models served via AWS's own Amazon Bedrock-backed Q Developer service; no support for arbitrary/OpenAI-compatible model endpoints or bring-your-own-API-key providers.

notes: Amazon Q Developer CLI (`q chat`) is AWS's Rust-based, Bedrock-backed agentic terminal coding agent, launched as an enhanced agentic experience in March 2025. It requires AWS Builder ID or IAM Identity Center sign-in (no API-key-only or account-free path), supports MCP, lifecycle hooks, and per-project `.amazonq/rules/` markdown rules, but lacks AGENTS.md support, subagent delegation, sandboxing, and structured JSON chat output. As of mid-2026 the open-source repo is no longer actively maintained: AWS is sunsetting Amazon Q Developer in favor of the backward-compatible, closed-source Kiro CLI (same `q`/`q chat` entry points, configs auto-migrated to `~/.kiro/`), with new signups blocked from May 2026 and full IDE/subscription end-of-support by April 2027.

pricing.comment: AWS lists a Free tier and Pro at $19/user/month. Free includes 50 agentic IDE/CLI requests/month; Pro increases limits and includes Java/.NET transformation capacity, with extra transformation lines billed at $0.003/LOC.

some_free_usage.comment: Free Tier includes a documented monthly allotment of agentic chat requests and code suggestions at no cost via AWS Builder ID.

no_account_required.comment: Requires signing in with an AWS Builder ID or IAM Identity Center/IAM credentials before any chat session; there is no local-model or account-free path.

can_use_other_subscriptions.comment: Models are served exclusively through AWS's own Amazon Bedrock-backed Q Developer service (Builder ID/IAM Identity Center auth); no option to route usage through a personal Anthropic/OpenAI API key or other vendor subscription.
